  1. Methods
  2. Model Architecture

Model Architecture

Overview

The model is a causally-masked transformer (SimpleTransformer) that maps a scalar time series of length \(L\) to a sequence of \(K\)-dimensional predictions — one prediction per timestep. The architecture has three main stages:

  1. Hermite embedding — lifts each scalar input to a polynomial feature vector
  2. Transformer encoder — processes the sequence with causal (autoregressive) attention
  3. Output projection — maps to the \(K\) prediction targets

Hermite Embedding

Instead of a learned linear input projection, each scalar \(X_t\) is embedded by evaluating the first \(d_{\text{model}}\) probabilist’s Hermite polynomials:

\[ H_n(x) \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, d_{\text{model}}-1 \]

defined by the recurrence \(H_0 = 1\), \(H_1 = x\), and:

\[ H_{n+1}(x) = x\,H_n(x) - n\,H_{n-1}(x) \]

With normalization, the embedding vector for input \(x\) is:

\[ \mathbf{e}(x) = \left(\frac{H_0(x)}{\sqrt{0!}},\;\frac{H_1(x)}{\sqrt{1!}},\;\ldots,\;\frac{H_{d-1}(x)}{\sqrt{(d-1)!}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^d \]

Why Hermite polynomials? They form an orthogonal basis of \(L^2(\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{N}(0,1))\) — the natural function space for Gaussian-distributed inputs. For OU process observations (which are marginally Gaussian by the fixed-variance construction), this provides a rich and well-conditioned feature space without requiring learned parameters.

Implementation

# src/model.py — HermiteEmbedding
class HermiteEmbedding(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, L, normalize=True):
        # precomputes sqrt(n!) normalization factors as a buffer
        ...

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len) or (batch, seq_len, 1)
        # returns: (batch, seq_len, L+1)
        ...

Positional Encoding

Standard sinusoidal positional encoding is added after the input projection:

\[ \text{PE}_{(t, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \quad \text{PE}_{(t, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \]

This encodes absolute position in the sequence, which is important for the model to know “how much data it has seen.”

Attention Blocks

Each AttentionBlock applies:

  1. Causal multi-head self-attention with a triangular upper mask (each position only attends to itself and earlier positions)
  2. Residual connection + LayerNorm
# src/model.py — AttentionBlock.forward
mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len) * float('-inf'), diagonal=1)
attn_out, attn_weights = self.attn(x, x, x, attn_mask=mask)
x = self.norm1(x + attn_out)

Causal masking is essential: at timestep \(t\), the model should only condition on \(X_1, \ldots, X_t\), never on future observations.

Feed-Forward Layer

After all attention blocks, a shared feed-forward network is applied:

\[ \text{FF}(x) = W_2 \,\text{ReLU}(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 \]

followed by a final residual connection and LayerNorm.

Output Projection

A single linear layer maps \(d_{\text{model}} \to K\) (= d_output = target.order = 3), producing the prediction for each of the \(K\) targets at every timestep.

Architecture Diagram

Input: (batch, L, 1)
         │
         ▼
  HermiteEmbedding          scalar → (batch, L, d_model)
         │
         ▼
  input_projection          linear, (d_model → d_model)
         │
         ▼
  PositionalEncoding        additive sinusoidal PE
         │
         ▼
  AttentionBlock × n_layers causal MHA + residual + norm
         │
         ▼
  FeedForward               (d_model → dim_feedforward → d_model)
         │
         ▼
  LayerNorm
         │
         ▼
  output_projection         linear, (d_model → d_output)
         │
         ▼
Output: (batch, L, K)

Hyperparameters

Configured in configs/model.yaml:

architecture:
  d_input: 1
  d_model: 20          # embedding / hidden dimension
  n_head: 1            # attention heads
  n_layers: 2          # number of AttentionBlocks
  max_len: 5000        # max sequence length for positional encoding
  dim_feedforward: 64  # inner dimension of the FF layer
  d_output: 3          # number of output cumulants (= target.order)
Parameter Value Notes
d_model 20 Also controls Hermite degree (\(L = d_{\text{model}} - 1 = 19\))
n_head 1 Single attention head
n_layers 2 Two stacked attention blocks
dim_feedforward 64 FF hidden size
d_output 3 Must match target.order in data.yaml

Parameter Count

With the default configuration, the model has a small number of parameters:

total_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
# Printed during training: e.g., ~5,000–15,000 parameters

The model is intentionally small to study what minimal capacity is needed to extract distributional information from OU trajectories.

Returning Attention Maps

The forward method returns both predictions and the list of attention weight tensors (one per layer), which are useful for visualization:

predictions, attention_maps = model(src)
# predictions:    (batch, L, K)
# attention_maps: list of (batch, n_head, L, L) tensors
